canonical_summary:
NRC pre-application engagement is the voluntary, non-statutory phase in which a prospective applicant works with the Office of Nuclear Reactor Regulation's Division of Advanced Reactors and Non-Power Production and Utilization Facilities (DANU) to resolve technical and licensing questions before a construction permit, combined license, standard design approval, or Part 53 application is docketed. It is structured around three formal mechanisms: pre-application white papers and technical reports, docketed topical reports reviewed under LIC-500 Revision 9, and readiness assessments. The entry artifact is typically a Regulatory Engagement Plan modeled on NEI's July 6, 2018 guideline (commonly cited as NEI 18-06), which sets meeting cadence, planned submittals, and a schedule.

The controlling staff guidance for non-LWR reviews is DANU-ISG-2022-01, the Roadmap finalized March 31, 2024, with companion ARCAP ISGs and Regulatory Guide 1.253 (April 3, 2024) endorsing NEI 21-07 Revision 1 for LMP-based applicants. Pre-application work has demonstrably compressed schedules: Kairos Hermes received the first non-LWR construction permit on December 12, 2023 after engagement starting November 2018; TerraPower Natrium's Kemmerer Unit 1 CP issued March 4, 2026; and the NRC published an 18-month CP review schedule for X-energy's Long Mott site explicitly citing pre-application effectiveness since 2018.
h1: NRC pre-application engagement for advanced reactors
meta_title: NRC pre-application engagement: the definitive guide
meta_description: How NRC pre-application engagement works for advanced reactors: REPs, topical reports, ARCAP, Part 53, durations, dockets, and how to compress timelines.

sections.heading: Why pre-application engagement decides your review schedule
paragraphs: Pre-application engagement is not required by statute or by 10 CFR Parts 50, 52, or 53. It is, however, the single most predictive variable for how long a docketed review takes and how many requests for additional information arrive after acceptance. The NRC's Office of Nuclear Reactor Regulation, through its Division of Advanced Reactors and Non-Power Production and Utilization Facilities (DANU), uses the pre-application phase to surface novel technical issues, agree on methodologies, and align on the content and structure of the eventual safety analysis report before the formal clock starts.

The agency has been explicit that effective pre-application work is the reason it can credit accelerated review schedules. When the staff published the 18-month construction permit review plan for X-energy's Long Mott Generating Station in June 2025, it pointed to continuous engagement with X-energy since September 2018 as the basis. Kairos Power's Hermes construction permit, the first non-LWR CP in more than fifty years, was issued December 12, 2023 after engagement that began in November 2018. TerraPower's Kemmerer Unit 1 CP, the first commercial non-LWR CP, was issued March 4, 2026 after more than four years of pre-application work.

The output of pre-application engagement is staff feedback, not approvals. Informal white papers do not produce binding NRC findings. The mechanism that does produce binding findings before a license application is the docketed topical report under LIC-500. This distinction governs how a developer should sequence work: novel methodology questions go to topical reports for formal closure; design and program details flow through white papers, audits, and meetings to converge on the SAR.

sections.heading: The formal flow: REP, white papers, topical reports, readiness assessments
paragraphs: To be listed as an active pre-applicant on NRC's vendor list, a developer must either submit a Regulatory Engagement Plan or demonstrate a substantial history of pre-application interactions. A letter of intent typically opens the relationship and declares scope, projected application date, and a commitment to engage throughout pre-application. Each active vendor is assigned a project number in the 99902-series (Kairos 99902069, X-energy 99902071, Holtec SMR-300 99902049, Natrium 99902100, Westinghouse AP300 99902111, Aalo 99902128, Last Energy 99902140, BWXT BANR 99902066, GE-Hitachi BWRX-300 99902067).

subsections.heading: Regulatory Engagement Plans
paragraphs: The Regulatory Engagement Plan concept originated with industry. NEI published Guidelines for Development of a Regulatory Engagement Plan on July 6, 2018, jointly with NRC and Nuclear Innovation Alliance input; the document is commonly referenced as NEI 18-06. The Holtec SMR-300 REP (ML22216A059) is a public model. A REP specifies desired meetings, planned pre-application submittals, and proposed timelines. NRC has been consistent that the REP is voluntary and the topics and depth are negotiated with staff.

subsections.heading: White papers and technical reports
paragraphs: White papers are the lightest mechanism. They request staff feedback on a specific technical or licensing question without formal docketing. They do not result in a safety evaluation, but they reduce the chance of late-stage surprises and let staff signal whether a planned approach is acceptable or needs revision before the developer commits to it in a topical report or application.

subsections.heading: Topical reports
paragraphs: Topical reports are standalone submittals reviewed under LIC-500 Revision 9 (January 27, 2022, ML20247G279). They produce a final safety evaluation that can be referenced verbatim in subsequent license applications. They are the only pre-application mechanism that yields binding NRC findings before a docketed license application.

subsections.heading: Readiness assessments
paragraphs: Readiness assessments give staff early visibility into draft application content, typically a draft PSAR, and let the NRC flag completeness or quality issues before acceptance review. X-energy and Dow's subsidiary Long Mott Energy used this mechanism in October 2023; NRC closed the assessment in January 2024 with no barriers identified, setting up the March 31, 2025 construction permit application.

sections.heading: Topical reports in depth
paragraphs: A topical report is the workhorse of pre-application engagement. It isolates one methodology, design feature, or analytical approach and asks the NRC staff to review and approve it independently of any specific plant. Once approved, the resulting -A version (the revised report, the RAIs and responses, and the staff safety evaluation, combined in a single document) can be referenced in a PSAR, FSAR, COL, SDA, or design certification, with the staff focusing its review on plant-specific conformance to the topical report's conditions and limitations.

LIC-500 Revision 9 defines four review pathways: Standard Review for highly complex new or revised TRs, with timelines of roughly two years; Compressed Review; Uncomplicated Review; and Safety Evaluation Confirmation Review. Each review proceeds through acceptance, one or more RAI rounds, a draft safety evaluation, a final safety evaluation, and issuance of the verified -A version. Until the -A is issued, the TR cannot be referenced in a docketed licensing action.

subsections.heading: Subject areas commonly addressed
bullets: Principal Design Criteria
Licensing Basis Event selection per NEI 18-04 and Regulatory Guide 1.233
Probabilistic Risk Assessment methodology
Fuel qualification, with NUREG-2246 as the consolidated guidance
Mechanistic source term methodology
SSC safety classification and special treatments
Accident analysis and dose methodology, often referencing RG 1.183
Quality assurance programs
Safeguards and security methodologies

subsections.heading: Worked examples
bullets: Kairos Power: more than eleven TRs since 2018, including KP-TR-011 Fuel Qualification Methodology (FSE ML23048A326) and KP-TR-012 Mechanistic Source Term (SE ML22340A628), all referenced in the Hermes PSAR submitted September 29, 2021.
NuScale: TR-0915-17565 Accident Source Term Methodology, final SE issued October 24, 2019, -A Revision 4 relied upon in the design certification (ML22287A155).

X-energy Xe-100: TRISO-X Pebble Fuel Qualification (April 2021, ML21246A289), Principal Design Criteria (ML24319A155), Mechanistic Source Term Approach (ML24131A149), Transient and Safety Analysis Methodology (ML24121A288), and Graphite (ML24309A123).

GE-Hitachi BWRX-300: NEDO-33910P-A Rev. 2 (Reactor Pressure Vessel Isolation and Overpressure Protection) and NEDO-33912P-A Rev. 1 (Stability), addressed in pre-application meetings January 24 and April 25, 2024.
BWXT BANR: QA Program Description BANR-QAPD-001, with NRC final safety evaluation issued January 31, 2024 (ML24113A192).
Terrestrial Energy: NRC issued a safety evaluation for the IMSR Postulated Initiating Events topical report in May 2026.

subsections.heading: Effect on the PSAR
paragraphs: 10 CFR 50.34(a) requires the PSAR to include principal design criteria, design bases, and accident analyses. When an applicant references an approved -A topical report, the staff treats the underlying SE findings as settled and reviews only plant-specific conformance: that the inputs are within the bounded envelope, that conditions and limitations are met, and that any deviations are justified. This is how a single topical-report review is leveraged across multiple PSARs, FSARs, COLs, and design approvals.

sections.heading: ARCAP, TICAP, RG 1.253, and the Part 53 transition
paragraphs: The Advanced Reactor Content of Application Project (ARCAP) is the NRC's technology-inclusive, risk-informed application guidance program for non-LWRs. The Technology-Inclusive Content of Application Project (TICAP) is its industry-led, DOE cost-shared counterpart. TICAP's principal deliverable is NEI 21-07, which covers the SAR portions that document outputs of the Licensing Modernization Project (LMP) methodology in NEI 18-04. ARCAP covers the remaining SAR chapters.

Regulatory Guide 1.253 Revision 0 was issued April 3, 2024 (ML23269A225). It endorses NEI 21-07 Revision 1 as one acceptable method for developing specified portions of a non-LWR SAR for applicants using NEI 18-04 (LMP). Scope of acceptance covers construction permits and operating licenses under 10 CFR Part 50 and combined licenses and design certifications under 10 CFR Part 52. The endorsement was without exceptions but with staff clarifications and additions.

The companion ARCAP Interim Staff Guidance suite issued in parallel on April 3, 2024 includes DANU-ISG-2022-01 (Roadmap, finalized March 31, 2024), DANU-ISG-2022-02 (Site Information), DANU-ISG-2022-03 (Routine Radioactive Effluents), DANU-ISG-2022-04 (Occupational Doses), DANU-ISG-2022-05 (Organization and Human-System Considerations), DANU-ISG-2022-06 (Post-Construction Inspection, Testing and Analysis Program), DANU-ISG-2022-07 (Risk-Informed ISI/IST), and DANU-ISG-2022-08 (LMP-Based Technical Specifications).

subsections.heading: Part 53 in 2026
paragraphs: The Part 53 final rule was published March 30, 2026 with corrections April 13, 2026 and an effective date of April 29, 2026. It establishes an optional third licensing pathway alongside Parts 50 and 52. Applications cannot be docketed before the effective date, but pre-application engagement is available now.

For pre-application engagement the practical implication is straightforward: a developer pursuing Part 50 or Part 52 today can lean on RG 1.253 plus the ARCAP ISGs to scope SAR content during pre-application; a developer planning a Part 53 application uses the Part 53 framework directly and treats LMP-derived guidance as supporting reference material. A proposed rule issued April 2, 2026 also streamlines commercial licensing for designs previously authorized under DOE or DOW programs, with related draft guidance under DANU-ISG-2026-XX.

sections.heading: Recent examples with durations and dockets

subsections.heading: Kairos Power KP-FHR (Docket 99902069)
paragraphs: Engagement began November 2018 and continues through 2026. Hermes 1 construction permit issued December 12, 2023; Hermes 2 construction permits issued November 21, 2024. 2024-2026 engagement has shifted to operating license readiness for Hermes 1 and topicals for the commercial KP-FHR.
bullets: Feb 20, 2024: Kairos signaled the planned safety analysis methodology topical report for Hermes 1 OL.
Sept 26, 2024: NRC audit plan for KP-FHR Safety Analysis Methodology (ML24269A202).
Sept 27, 2024: pre-submittal meeting on the Safety Analysis Methodology topical report (ML24270A087).
Dec 2025: continued staff evaluation work (ML25344A109).
Duration: roughly 7+ years and still active during CP execution.

subsections.heading: X-energy Xe-100 (Docket 99902071)
paragraphs: Engagement began September 2018. A readiness assessment opened in October 2023 and closed in January 2024 with no barriers identified. Long Mott Energy LLC, a Dow subsidiary, filed a four-unit Xe-100 construction permit application on March 31, 2025; NRC made it public May 5, 2025 and docketed it in mid-July 2025.
bullets: Feb 2024 engagement table (ML24054A842): LBE methodologies, source term, defense-in-depth, PRA.
June 2025: NRC published an 18-month CP review schedule (final SER by December 2026) explicitly crediting pre-application effectiveness.
Duration: roughly 6.5 years from September 2018 to CPA tender.

subsections.heading: TerraPower Natrium (Docket 99902100)
paragraphs: More than four years of pre-application work. Kemmerer Unit 1 Part 50 CPA submitted March 2024 and docketed May 2024. NRC completed its safety review in December 2025; the Commission voted to issue the construction permit on March 4, 2026, making Kemmerer the first commercial non-LWR CP. TerraPower began safety-related construction April 23, 2026.

subsections.heading: NuScale US460 (Docket NRC-2023-0027)
paragraphs: Standard Design Approval application submitted under 10 CFR Part 52 in late 2023. NRC issued the SDA on May 29, 2025, completing review in under two years. NuScale remains the only SMR vendor with both a certified US600 design (2023) and an approved US460 SDA.

subsections.heading: Holtec SMR-300 (Docket 99902049)
paragraphs: Pre-application work since 2019, with an earlier suspended SMR-160 effort. Holtec's public Regulatory Engagement Plan (ML22216A059) is a widely cited model. Holtec submitted Part 1 of a Part 50 CPA for two SMR-300 units at the Palisades site in late 2025, partially docketed in early 2026.

subsections.heading: Aalo Atomics Aalo-1 (Docket 99902128)
bullets: July 1, 2024: REP submitted (ML24193A003) for a seven-unit Aalo-1 pod COLA at the Idaho Nuclear Project.
April 23, 2025 public meeting (ML25112A121); follow-on REP discussion (ML25113A048).
Dec 2025: design overview and cybersecurity meetings (ML25336A296, ML25344A073).
Duration so far: roughly 24 months, with COLA targeted 2026.

subsections.heading: GE-Hitachi BWRX-300 (Docket 99902067)
bullets: Jan 24, 2024 meeting (ML24039A081): piping break exclusion under SRP BTP 3-3 and 3-4 tied to NEDO-33910P-A Rev. 2.
April 25, 2024 meeting (ML24172A107): stability and GDC 12 compliance for NEDO-33912P-A Rev. 1, with CNSC and ONR participating.
April 28, 2025: TVA submitted a partial CP application for Clinch River Unit 1 (Docket 50-05900); review target December 2026.
Duration: roughly 6 years before CP tender.

subsections.heading: BWXT BANR (Docket 99902066)
bullets: Aug 2022: REP submitted (ML22243A112).
Jan 31, 2024: NRC final safety evaluation for BANR-QAPD-001 Rev. 0 (ML24113A192).
Jan 2024: UN TRISO Fuel Qualification Plan (ML24004A173).
Duration: 3.5+ years, still in topical-report mode without a docketed application.

sections.heading: How to compress pre-application engagement
paragraphs: Pre-application is compressible but not skippable. The NRC has accelerated reviews when the application is clean, the methodology is settled, and the topical reports are already approved. Several patterns recur in the records.

First, sequence topical reports to close out the highest-novelty methodology issues first: source term, fuel qualification, LBE selection, PRA, and SSC safety classification. Methodology approval before PSAR submission is what lets the staff treat those chapters as settled and focus on plant-specific conformance.

Second, treat the REP as a real schedule, not boilerplate. The REP should commit to specific meeting dates, named topical reports with submission targets, and audit windows. The Holtec SMR-300 REP (ML22216A059) is the canonical public example.

Third, use readiness assessments before formal acceptance review. The X-energy / Long Mott readiness assessment opened October 2023 and closed January 2024 with no barriers; the resulting CPA was published in May 2025 and docketed in July 2025, with NRC publishing an 18-month review schedule.

Fourth, write to RG 1.253 and the ARCAP ISGs from day one if pursuing a non-LWR under Part 50 or Part 52. Map the SAR table of contents to TICAP (LMP outputs) and ARCAP (site, effluents, occupational dose, organization, ITAAC, tech specs). This avoids re-litigating SRP applicability and lets the staff use the same content guidance internally.

Fifth, hold quarterly cadence at minimum during the heavy methodology phase, and publish meeting summaries to ADAMS. Pre-application is a written-record process; informal conversations that are not memorialized do not carry forward to the review.

sections.heading: Where AI agents help
paragraphs: Pre-application engagement is fundamentally a document-and-record exercise: REPs, topical reports, RAIs, safety evaluations, meeting summaries, and audit reports, all indexed in ADAMS by accession number. The work product compounds: an RAI on Kairos's fuel qualification topical sets a precedent that an X-energy or BWXT TRISO TR will be measured against. Most of the staff hours on the applicant side are spent reading prior records, cross-referencing them against the current design, and drafting submittals that anticipate likely RAIs.

AI agents are well-suited to four jobs in this loop. First, ADAMS retrieval and cross-referencing: pulling every meeting summary, audit plan, and safety evaluation under a given vendor's project number and indexing them by topic. Second, RAI prediction: comparing a draft topical report against prior approved -A versions and the RAIs that produced them to surface the likely staff questions before submission. Third, gap analysis: mapping a design against the SRP, RG 1.253, the ARCAP ISGs, and NEI 18-04 to identify exemption, alternative-approach, or topical-report requirements. Fourth, SER conformance checking: verifying that a PSAR draft stays within the conditions and limitations of every -A topical report it references.

None of this replaces the judgment of a regulatory affairs lead or the substantive engineering. It compresses the read-and-cross-reference layer that today consumes the bulk of pre-application time, and it makes the written record traceable in a way that is auditable by both the developer and the staff.

faqs.question: Is pre-application engagement required by NRC regulations?
faqs.answer: No. The NRC has no statutory or regulatory requirement to engage in pre-application activity before submitting a construction permit, combined license, standard design approval, design certification, or Part 53 application. Engagement is voluntary and is structured through three formal mechanisms: white papers and technical reports, docketed topical reports under LIC-500, and readiness assessments. In practice, the agency treats substantive pre-application engagement as the predicate for accelerated review schedules. The NRC explicitly credited continuous engagement with X-energy since September 2018 when it published the 18-month construction permit review schedule for Long Mott in June 2025. Skipping pre-application is legally permitted but operationally unwise for any first-of-a-kind design.

faqs.question: How long does pre-application engagement typically take?
faqs.answer: Duration scales with technological novelty. Light-water SMRs and HTGRs have run roughly six years before construction permit tender: GE-Hitachi BWRX-300 engaged from 2019 to TVA's April 2025 partial CPA, and X-energy Xe-100 ran from September 2018 to Long Mott's March 2025 CPA. Fluoride-salt-cooled high-temperature reactors took about five years to first CP, with Kairos engaging from November 2018 to the Hermes CP on December 12, 2023. Sodium fast reactors followed a similar arc; TerraPower's Natrium took more than four years to the March 4, 2026 Kemmerer Unit 1 CP. Microreactors such as Aalo Atomics are one to two years into REP-stage work.

faqs.question: What is the difference between a white paper and a topical report?
faqs.answer: A white paper requests staff feedback on a specific technical or licensing question without formal docketing. It does not produce a safety evaluation and does not bind the NRC. It is appropriate for early framing, for confirming that a planned approach is acceptable, and for resolving narrow questions cheaply. A topical report is a standalone submittal reviewed under LIC-500 Revision 9 (January 27, 2022, ML20247G279). It is docketed, goes through acceptance review, RAIs, draft and final safety evaluations, and culminates in an approved -A version that combines the revised report, the RAIs and responses, and the staff safety evaluation. The -A version can be referenced in subsequent license applications with the staff focusing only on plant-specific conformance to its conditions and limitations.

faqs.question: What is a Regulatory Engagement Plan and do I need one?
faqs.answer: A Regulatory Engagement Plan, or REP, is a vendor-prepared document that lays out the scope of pre-application activity, planned meetings, planned topical and white paper submittals, and a timeline. The REP concept originated in industry through NEI's Guidelines for Development of a Regulatory Engagement Plan published July 6, 2018, commonly cited as NEI 18-06. The NRC does not require a REP. To be listed as an active pre-applicant on the NRC vendor page, however, a developer must either submit a REP or demonstrate a substantial history of interactions. The Holtec SMR-300 REP filed in 2022 (ML22216A059) is the canonical public example and a workable template for structure.

faqs.question: Should I file under Part 50, Part 52, or Part 53?
faqs.answer: The Part 53 final rule was published March 30, 2026 with corrections April 13, 2026 and an effective date of April 29, 2026. Applications cannot be docketed before the effective date, although pre-application engagement is available. Part 53 is risk-informed, performance-based, and technology-inclusive by rule rather than by guidance overlay. Part 50 (construction permit / operating license) and Part 52 (combined license, standard design approval, design certification) remain available and are the only paths with extensive precedent for non-LWRs today. For LMP-based non-LWR designs targeting Part 50 or Part 52, Regulatory Guide 1.253 (April 3, 2024) endorses NEI 21-07 Revision 1 and is paired with the ARCAP Interim Staff Guidance suite. Choice of part is a function of schedule, design maturity, and tolerance for being a first mover under Part 53.

faqs.question: What is the difference between ARCAP and TICAP?
faqs.answer: ARCAP, the Advanced Reactor Content of Application Project, is the NRC-led program that produces technology-inclusive content guidance for non-LWR safety analysis reports. Its output is the ARCAP Interim Staff Guidance suite under the DANU designation, including DANU-ISG-2022-01 (Roadmap), 2022-02 (Site), 2022-03 (Effluents), 2022-04 (Occupational Doses), 2022-05 (Organization and Human-System Considerations), 2022-06 (ITAAC), 2022-07 (Risk-Informed ISI/IST), and 2022-08 (Tech Specs). TICAP, the Technology-Inclusive Content of Application Project, is the industry-led, DOE cost-shared companion. Its principal deliverable is NEI 21-07, which addresses the SAR portions that document the outputs of the Licensing Modernization Project methodology in NEI 18-04. RG 1.253 endorses NEI 21-07 Revision 1 as one acceptable method.

faqs.question: Can NRC findings during pre-application bind a later license review?
faqs.answer: Only topical reports produce binding NRC findings before a docketed license application. The final safety evaluation issued on an approved -A topical report can be referenced verbatim in a PSAR, FSAR, COL, SDA, or design certification, and the staff will treat the underlying findings as settled. White papers and meeting summaries do not produce binding findings; they record the staff's preliminary views and reduce the chance of surprises but do not foreclose later positions. The implication for sequencing is that novel methodology questions, such as mechanistic source term, fuel qualification, PRA methodology, and SSC safety classification, should be moved into topical reports early so that they are closed by the time the application is submitted.

faqs.question: What does the docket project number 99902-series mean?
faqs.answer: The 99902-series is the NRC's provisional docket number range for pre-application engagement. Each active vendor or design is assigned a project number that organizes all pre-application correspondence, meeting summaries, audit plans, and topical reports in ADAMS. Current examples include Kairos Power KP-FHR 99902069, BWXT BANR 99902066, GE-Hitachi BWRX-300 99902067, X-energy Xe-100 99902071, Holtec SMR-300 99902049, TerraPower Natrium 99902100, Westinghouse AP300 99902111, Aalo Atomics 99902128, and Last Energy PWR-20 99902140. Once a license application is submitted, the project transitions to a permanent docket number (for example, TVA's Clinch River Unit 1 BWRX-300 partial CP application is at Docket 50-05900).

faqs.question: How do approved topical reports interact with the PSAR?
faqs.answer: 10 CFR 50.34(a) requires a PSAR to include the principal design criteria, design bases, accident analyses, and other technical content. When an applicant references an approved -A topical report in its PSAR, the staff treats the underlying safety findings as settled. The PSAR review focuses on plant-specific conformance: confirming that inputs are within the approved bounding parameters, that the topical report's conditions and limitations are met, and that any deviations are justified. This is the central efficiency mechanism of the topical report process. Kairos's Hermes PSAR submitted September 29, 2021 referenced multiple Kairos topical reports, including KP-TR-011 (Fuel Qualification Methodology) and KP-TR-012 (Mechanistic Source Term), and the construction permit issued December 12, 2023.

faqs.question: What is a readiness assessment and when should I request one?
faqs.answer: A readiness assessment is a pre-application mechanism in which NRC staff get early visibility into a draft application package, typically a draft PSAR or COL, and identify completeness or quality issues before formal acceptance review. It is not a safety evaluation. X-energy and Long Mott Energy used a readiness assessment that opened in October 2023 and closed in January 2024 with no barriers identified, which set up the March 31, 2025 construction permit application and the subsequent 18-month review schedule. Readiness assessments are most valuable when the draft application is roughly 80 to 90 percent complete, the topical reports it relies on are approved or near approval, and the applicant wants to validate scope and structure before formal submission.

faqs.question: Which guidance documents do I need to follow for a non-LWR pre-application?
faqs.answer: The core stack for an LMP-based non-LWR pursuing Part 50 or Part 52 is: NEI 18-04 Revision 1 (LMP methodology), endorsed with clarifications by Regulatory Guide 1.233 Revision 0 (June 9, 2020, ML20091L698); NEI 21-07 Revision 1 (TICAP SAR content), endorsed by Regulatory Guide 1.253 Revision 0 (April 3, 2024, ML23269A225); DANU-ISG-2022-01 Roadmap (finalized March 31, 2024, ML23297A158) and the companion ARCAP ISGs 2022-02 through 2022-08; LIC-500 Revision 9 (January 27, 2022, ML20247G279) for topical report process; and NUREG-2246 for fuel qualification. The NRC explicitly states that DANU-ISG-2022-01, particularly its environmental sections, generally applies to all reactor technologies, not only LMP-based non-LWRs.

faqs.question: How does Part 53 change pre-application engagement?
faqs.answer: Part 53 reduces the need to negotiate technology-inclusivity issue-by-issue because the rule itself encodes the risk-informed, performance-based outcomes that ARCAP and TICAP previously had to layer onto Parts 50 and 52 through guidance. The mechanics of pre-application engagement, including REPs, white papers, topical reports under LIC-500, and readiness assessments, remain the same. Applications cannot be docketed before April 29, 2026, but pre-application work is available now. A developer choosing Part 53 will follow a separate guidance hierarchy keyed to the new subparts and treat NEI 18-04 and NEI 21-07 as supporting reference material rather than primary content guidance. A proposed rule issued April 2, 2026 also streamlines commercial licensing for designs previously authorized under DOE or DOW programs.

citations.label: NEI press release on REP guidelines (July 2018)
Part 53 final rule Federal Register notice (March 30, 2026)
Kairos Hermes Construction Permit press release (Dec 2023)
